verses.commentary: Kingdom like ten virgins (parthenoi, παρθένοι) taking lamps to meet the bridegroom. Wedding procession imagery — bridesmaids awaiting the groom's late-night arrival to escort him to the wedding feast.
Five wise (phronimoi, φρόνιμοι, prudent), five foolish (morai, μωραί). The division precedes the test. Wisdom is preparation; folly is improvisation.
Foolish took lamps without oil. The visible apparatus is in place; the fuel is missing. Form without substance.
Wise took flasks of oil with lamps. The reserve makes the difference under delay.
Bridegroom delayed; all slept. Sleep is not the issue — both groups slept. Delay reveals preparation.
Midnight cry: 'The bridegroom!' Come out to meet him! Sudden, unanticipated arrival.
All virgins rise and trim their lamps. Activity is identical at the alarm. The fuel question now becomes urgent.
Foolish to wise: 'Give us some of your oil; our lamps are going out.' Petition exposes the absence too late.
'No — there may not be enough for us and you. Go and buy.' Some preparations cannot be lent at the last moment. Personal readiness is not transferable.
While they went to buy, the bridegroom came. The ready entered the feast; the door was shut (ekleisthe he thyra, ἐκλείσθη ἡ θύρα). Doors close in eschatology.
Later: 'Lord, Lord, open to us!' Echoes 7:21-23. Late-arriving claims to relationship.
'Truly I tell you, I do not know you.' The condemnation: relational unfamiliarity, not procedural failure. Compare 7:23.
'Watch, for you know neither the day nor the hour.' Application restated. Watch = be prepared regardless of timing.
Talents parable: man going on a journey, entrusts wealth to servants. The departure-entrustment-return shape characterizes parables of accountability.

Five talents, two talents, one talent — distributed 'according to ability' (dynamis, δύναμις). Talent (talanton, τάλαντον) — large sum, possibly 6000 denarii each. Differentiated stewardship, not equal distribution.
Five-talent servant immediately traded, made five more. Activity, doubling. Productivity through risk.
Two-talent servant likewise: gained two more. Same return rate; smaller absolute gain. Proportional faithfulness.
One-talent servant dug a hole and hid the master's money. The conservative play. Preservation without growth.
After 'a long time' (chronon polyn, χρόνον πολύν) — delay theme again — the master returns and settles accounts. Reckoning comes.
Five-talent servant: 'See, I have made five more.' Reports gains.

Master: 'Well done, good and faithful servant. You have been faithful in a little; I will set you over much. Enter into the joy of your master.' Reward triplet: praise, promotion, participation in joy. Faithfulness scales upward.
Two-talent servant reports gains. Lesser absolute amount.
Master gives identical commendation. Reward is not proportional to absolute return but to faithfulness in given allotment. The grace evens the math.
One-talent servant: 'I knew you were a hard man, reaping where you did not sow.' Begins with accusation. Theological framing of his inaction: fear of an unjust master.
'I was afraid; I hid your talent.' Fear as paralysis-cause. The diagnosis the servant offers is the indictment.
Master: 'Wicked and lazy (poneros kai okneros, πονηρὸς καὶ ὀκνηρός) servant! You knew that — ' He grants the premise without endorsing it. The servant's own theology condemns him.
'You ought at least to have put the money with bankers — interest on return.' Minimum effort would have sufficed. Even risk-averse stewardship has a productive form.
Talent taken from him, given to the one with ten. Resources reallocate to the productive.
'To everyone who has, more will be given... from him who has not, even what he has will be taken.' Compounding principle (compare 13:12). Use determines retention.
Worthless servant cast into outer darkness — weeping and gnashing of teeth. Inaction is condemned alongside active wickedness. Hiding is not safe.
'When the Son of Man comes in his glory, all the angels with him' — eschatological coronation scene. Sits on the throne of glory (kathisei epi thronou doxes, καθίσει ἐπὶ θρόνου δόξης).
'All nations (panta ta ethne, πάντα τὰ ἔθνη) gathered before him.' Universal judgment. Separates as shepherd separates sheep from goats. Pastoral metaphor for forensic act.
Sheep on right, goats on left. Cultural significance: right is the position of honor; left of disfavor. The separation precedes the verdict.
'Come, you blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world.' Pre-creation grace. The kingdom is gift, not earned — though grace's evidence is what follows.
'I was hungry and you gave me food, thirsty and you gave me drink, a stranger and you welcomed me.' Six categories of need: hunger, thirst, alienation.
'Naked and you clothed me, sick and you visited me, in prison and you came.' Three more: nakedness, sickness, incarceration. Six total — comprehensive mercy categories.
Righteous puzzled: 'When did we see you hungry...?' They were not strategically benefitting Jesus; they were responding to human need.
Repeated category list. Their service was unselfconscious. They did not perform mercy for theological credit.
Sick and prison repeated. The repetition emphasizes their unawareness. Genuine mercy does not keep ledgers.

'As you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me.' Christological identification with the vulnerable. 'These my brothers' debated — possibly disciples specifically, possibly all suffering. The judgment turns on treatment of the marginal.
To those on the left: 'Depart, accursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.' The fire was for demons; humans share it by joining their refusal.
Same six categories — but in negative. 'You gave me no food, no drink.' Sins of omission. Inaction is not neutrality.
'A stranger and you did not welcome, naked and not clothed, sick and in prison and you did not visit.' Comprehensive failure to engage need.
Goats ask: 'When did we see you...?' Same surprise as righteous. Both groups were unaware of the cosmic significance of their actions.
'As you did not to one of the least of these — you did not to me.' Christ-identification with the neglected. Omission is rejection of Christ.
These to eternal punishment (kolasin aionion, κόλασιν αἰώνιον), the righteous to eternal life (zoen aionion, ζωὴν αἰώνιον). Symmetric finality. Final destinies are equally eternal in duration.
